schools.summary: The aged, blind Isaac calls Esau: 'and it came to pass, that when Isaac was old, and his eyes were dim, so that he could not see, he called Esau his eldest son, and said unto him, My son: and he said unto him, Behold, here am I' — Isaac, now old (about a hundred and thirty-seven) and blind, could not see (the infirmity of age, the blindness that the deception would exploit, vv.21-23, the setting for the blessing); Isaac calling Esau 'his eldest son' — purposing to bless him (vv.2-4, the father's partiality, 25:28, and his intent to give the chief blessing to Esau, the calling of the firstborn); Esau's answer, 'Behold, here am I,' showing his ready attendance on his father (the setting for the charge, vv.2-4, the son called to the blessing) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass, that when Isaac was old, and his eyes were dim, so that he could not see, he called Esau his eldest son
the aged, blind Isaac calling Esau
the infirmity of age, the blindness the deception would exploit (vv.21-23)
the father purposing to bless his firstborn (25:28)

schools.summary: Isaac's sense of approaching death: 'and he said, Behold now, I am old, I know not the day of my death' — Isaac, conscious of his age and the nearness of death, purposing to bless his son before he died (the uncertainty of the day of death prompting the blessing now, the occasion of the patriarchal blessing, the solemn bestowal before death, as Jacob later, ch. 49); Isaac's sense of mortality — though he lived many years yet (he died at a hundred and eighty, 35:28), he felt death near (the prompting to set his house in order, the bestowal of the blessing as a dying act); Isaac's words introducing the solemn patriarchal blessing — the conveying of the covenant and the chief inheritance to his son before death (the gravity of the act, the setting for the charge to Esau, vv.3-4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he said, Behold now, I am old, I know not the day of my death
Isaac's sense of approaching death
the uncertainty of death prompting the blessing now
the solemn patriarchal blessing before death (cf. ch. 49)

schools.summary: Isaac sends Esau to hunt venison: 'now therefore take, I pray thee, thy weapons, thy quiver and thy bow, and go out to the field, and take me some venison' — Isaac bidding Esau take his hunting weapons and bring venison (the charge to the hunter, Esau the man of the field, 25:27, the savory meat Isaac loved, 25:28, the preparation for the blessing); the weapons of the hunter — Esau the skilled hunter (25:27) sent to take game (the venison that Isaac loved, 25:28, the occasion of the blessing tied to the meat); 'take me some venison' (Isaac would eat of Esau's venison before blessing him, the partiality, loving Esau for his venison, 25:28, the savory meat the occasion of the blessing, the charge that Rebekah overheard, v.5) — Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: Now therefore take, I pray thee, thy weapons, thy quiver and thy bow, and go out to the field, and take me some venison
Isaac sending Esau to hunt venison
the hunter (25:27) sent for the savory meat Isaac loved (25:28)
the charge that Rebekah overheard (v.5)

schools.summary: Isaac's purpose to bless Esau (against the oracle): 'and make me savoury meat, such as I love, and bring it to me, that I may eat; that my soul may bless thee before I die' — Isaac would eat of the venison (the savory meat he loved, 25:28) before blessing Esau (the partiality and the appetite, the love of Esau for his venison, the meal preceding the blessing); Isaac purposing 'that my soul may bless thee before I die' — to give Esau the chief, patriarchal blessing (the covenant and the inheritance), yet this was to prefer the elder to the younger, contrary to the oracle (25:23, the elder shall serve the younger), Calvin noting that Isaac, swayed by his love for Esau (25:28), contends against the oracle of God — purposing to give the blessing to the elder, whom God had not chosen (the fault of the patriarch, acting against God's revealed word, whether through forgetfulness, partiality, or unbelief, the providence that would overrule his intent, vv.30-33); the patriarchal blessing ('my soul may bless thee') a solemn, prophetic act — conveying the covenant-promise and the chief inheritance (Isaac's purpose to bestow it on Esau, against the oracle, setting the stage for Rebekah's scheme and the deception, vv.5-29, God's purpose, the younger preferred, would stand despite Isaac's intent) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: How is Isaac's purpose to bless Esau to be judged?

sides.position: Isaac, swayed by partiality (25:28), contends against the oracle of God - purposing the chief blessing for Esau the elder, against the word that the elder should serve the younger (25:23); the fault God would overrule (vv.30-33)

themes: And make me savoury meat, such as I love... that my soul may bless thee before I die
Isaac's purpose to bless Esau (the crux of acting against the oracle)
Isaac, swayed by partiality (25:28), contending against the oracle (25:23)
the fault of the patriarch, which God would overrule (vv.30-33)

schools.summary: Rebekah overhears Isaac's charge: 'and Rebekah heard when Isaac spake to Esau his son. And Esau went to the field to hunt for venison, and to bring it' — Rebekah overhearing Isaac's charge to Esau (vv.2-4, the providence, and Rebekah's attention, by which she learned of Isaac's purpose, the occasion of her scheme, vv.6-17, the mother set on securing the blessing for Jacob); Esau going to hunt — giving Rebekah the opportunity (while Esau was away) to carry out her plan (the time afforded for the deception, the providence, and the scheme, at work); Esau's going to hunt giving Rebekah her window (the mother, mindful of the oracle, 25:23, yet resolved on unlawful means, planning to clothe Jacob and forestall Esau, the scheme begun, the deception set in motion) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB, Calvin.

themes: And Rebekah heard when Isaac spake to Esau his son. And Esau went to the field to hunt for venison, and to bring it
Rebekah overhearing Isaac's charge
the mother set on securing the blessing for Jacob (vv.6-17)
the opportunity afforded while Esau was away, the scheme begun

schools.summary: Rebekah discloses the plan to Jacob: 'and Rebekah spake unto Jacob her son, saying, Behold, I heard thy father speak unto Esau thy brother, saying' — Rebekah telling Jacob what she had overheard (Isaac's purpose to bless Esau, vv.2-4, the mother disclosing the plan to her favored son, 25:28, the beginning of the scheme to secure the blessing for Jacob by deception); Rebekah reporting Isaac's words — informing Jacob of the danger (that Esau would be blessed, the mother's resolve to forestall it, the disclosure that set the deception in motion); Rebekah's telling Jacob beginning the plan (vv.7-10) — to clothe Jacob as Esau and have him bring the meat and receive the blessing (the mother, mindful of the oracle, 25:23, yet using unlawful means, the scheme that mingled faith in the promise with the sin of deceit) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Wesley.

themes: And Rebekah spake unto Jacob her son, saying, Behold, I heard thy father speak unto Esau thy brother
Rebekah disclosing the plan to Jacob
the mother informing her favored son (25:28) of Isaac's purpose
the scheme mingling faith in the promise with the sin of deceit

schools.summary: Rebekah reports Isaac's purpose to bless before the LORD: 'bring me venison, and make me savoury meat, that I may eat, and bless thee before the LORD before my death' — Rebekah repeating Isaac's charge (vv.3-4), noting that he would bless Esau 'before the LORD' (in God's presence, as a solemn, sacred act, the gravity of the blessing, the report that moved Jacob to act); the phrase 'before the LORD' — the patriarchal blessing a solemn, religious act, done in God's presence and name (a prophetic conveying of the covenant, the seriousness of the matter, the blessing Rebekah was resolved Jacob should have); 'before my death' (Isaac's purpose to bless before he died, vv.2, 4, the urgency that moved Rebekah's scheme, the blessing to be secured before Esau returned and before Isaac died, the occasion of the deception) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: Bring me venison, and make me savoury meat, that I may eat, and bless thee before the LORD before my death
Rebekah reporting Isaac's purpose to bless before the LORD
the patriarchal blessing a solemn, sacred act done in God's presence
the urgency that moved Rebekah's scheme

schools.summary: Rebekah commands Jacob's obedience: 'now therefore, my son, obey my voice according to that which I command thee' — Rebekah commanding Jacob to obey her in the scheme she was about to lay out (vv.9-10, the mother's authority pressed, the demand for obedience to the plan, the resolve of Rebekah to carry out the deception); Rebekah pressing her authority ('obey my voice') — requiring Jacob to follow her plan (the mother taking the lead in the scheme, the command that drew Jacob into the deception, despite his misgiving, vv.11-12); 'according to that which I command thee' (Rebekah's plan, vv.9-10, to be obeyed, the mother directing the deception, the scheme pressed on Jacob, the obedience that, while honoring the mother, drew him into sin) — Gill.

themes: Now therefore, my son, obey my voice according to that which I command thee
Rebekah commanding Jacob's obedience to her plan
the mother taking the lead in the scheme
the obedience that, while honoring the mother, drew Jacob into sin

schools.summary: Rebekah's plan to make savory meat from the kids: 'go now to the flock, and fetch me from thence two good kids of the goats; and I will make them savoury meat for thy father, such as he loveth' — Rebekah bidding Jacob fetch two kids, which she would dress as the savory meat Isaac loved (in place of Esau's venison, the device — kids prepared to taste like venison, the mother's part in the deception, making the meat, the plan to deceive Isaac's palate); the two kids — enough for the meat (and the skins, v.16, for the disguise, the mother's skill in dressing them as venison, the substitute for the game Esau would bring); 'such as he loveth' (Rebekah would make the meat as Isaac loved it, deceiving his taste, the part of the scheme that fooled the blind, aged father, the device prepared, the meat and, with the skins and raiment, the full disguise, vv.15-16, the deception advancing) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: Go now to the flock, and fetch me from thence two good kids of the goats; and I will make them savoury meat for thy father, such as he loveth
Rebekah's plan to make savory meat from the kids
the kids dressed as venison to deceive Isaac's taste
the device prepared (the meat and, with the skins, the disguise, v.16)

schools.summary: Rebekah's design that Jacob receive the blessing: 'and thou shalt bring it to thy father, that he may eat, and that he may bless thee before his death' — Rebekah's design: Jacob to bring the meat to Isaac, that he might eat and bless Jacob (mistaking him for Esau) before his death (the aim of the scheme — to secure the patriarchal blessing for Jacob, the deception's purpose); Isaac would eat the meat (thinking it Esau's venison) — the deception of his taste (the meal preceding the blessing, the scheme working through the father's appetite); 'that he may bless thee before his death' (the goal of the plan: the blessing secured for Jacob, the mother resolved that the oracle's blessing, 25:23, should be Jacob's, yet by unlawful means, the design that mingled the right end, Jacob the heir, with the wrong means, deceit) — Gill.

themes: And thou shalt bring it to thy father, that he may eat, and that he may bless thee before his death
Rebekah's design that Jacob receive the blessing
the goal of the scheme - the blessing (25:23) secured for Jacob
the design mingling the right end (Jacob the heir) with the wrong means (deceit)

schools.summary: Jacob's misgiving about the deception: 'and Jacob said to Rebekah his mother, Behold, Esau my brother is a hairy man, and I am a smooth man' — Jacob raising the practical difficulty: Esau was hairy (25:25), and Jacob smooth (if Isaac felt him, the deception would be discovered, the obstacle to the scheme, Jacob's misgiving — not at the sin of the deceit, but at the danger of detection); the physical contrast — Esau hairy, Jacob smooth (the obstacle the disguise, the goat-skins, v.16, would address, the practical problem Jacob foresaw); Jacob's misgiving at the risk of being found out, not at the wrong of lying — a sad mark of the conscience more troubled by detection than by sin (the patriarch, and the godly, not free of the fault, the objection that Rebekah answered with the disguise, v.16, and the taking of the curse, v.13) — Gill, JFB, Calvin.

themes: And Jacob said to Rebekah his mother, Behold, Esau my brother is a hairy man, and I am a smooth man
Jacob's misgiving about the deception
the obstacle of the hair (Esau hairy, Jacob smooth, 25:25), addressed by the disguise (v.16)
the conscience troubled by detection, not by the sin of the lie

schools.summary: Jacob's fear of being found a deceiver: 'my father peradventure will feel me, and I shall seem to him as a deceiver; and I shall bring a curse upon me, and not a blessing' — Jacob fearing that, if Isaac felt him (and found him smooth, v.11), he would be discovered as a deceiver (a mocker, an impostor, the dread of detection, the practical objection to the scheme, the fear, again, of being found out); 'a deceiver' (a mocker, one who deludes) — what Jacob feared to be taken for (the shame and danger of detection, the obstacle to the plan); 'I shall bring a curse upon me, and not a blessing' (Jacob fearing that, if discovered, Isaac would curse him, instead of blessing, the dread of the curse, the fear that the scheme would backfire, the objection Rebekah answered by taking the curse on herself, v.13) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: My father peradventure will feel me, and I shall seem to him as a deceiver; and I shall bring a curse upon me, and not a blessing
Jacob's fear of being found a deceiver
the dread of detection (if Isaac felt him, v.11)
the fear of a curse, which Rebekah answered by taking it on herself (v.13)

schools.summary: Rebekah takes the curse upon herself: 'and his mother said unto him, Upon me be thy curse, my son: only obey my voice, and go fetch me them' — Rebekah, to overcome Jacob's fear of a curse (v.12), taking the curse upon herself (the mother's confident assumption of the risk, whether from faith, sure of the oracle, 25:23, that the blessing was Jacob's, and that no curse would fall, or from rashness, presuming on the end while using sinful means, the bold pledge); Rebekah's confidence — she was so sure the blessing belonged to Jacob (by the oracle, 25:23) that she feared no curse, yet her means (deceit) were wrong (the faith in the promise mingled with the sin of the device, the mother taking the responsibility); 'only obey my voice, and go fetch me them' (Rebekah pressing Jacob to obey and fetch the kids, v.9, the mother overruling his objection, the scheme pressed forward, the deception carried on, the right end of the oracle pursued by the wrong means, which the witnesses do not excuse) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And his mother said unto him, Upon me be thy curse, my son: only obey my voice, and go fetch me them
Rebekah taking the curse upon herself
the confidence in the oracle (25:23) - whether faith or rashness
the faith in the promise mingled with the wrong means of deceit

schools.summary: Jacob fetches the kids, Rebekah prepares the meat: 'and he went, and fetched, and brought them to his mother: and his mother made savoury meat, such as his father loved' — Jacob, overcome by his mother's pledge (v.13), obeying: he fetching the two kids (v.9), and Rebekah dressing them as the savory meat Isaac loved (the scheme carried out, the mother's part in preparing the deceptive meal, the deception advancing); Jacob obeying — fetching the kids (the son drawn into the deception by the mother's command and pledge, the scheme proceeding); 'his mother made savoury meat, such as his father loved' (Rebekah dressing the kids as venison, v.9, as Isaac loved it, the deception of the taste prepared, the meal ready, the disguise to follow, vv.15-16) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he went, and fetched, and brought them to his mother: and his mother made savoury meat, such as his father loved
Jacob fetching the kids and Rebekah preparing the meat
the son drawn into the deception by the mother's pledge (v.13)
the deceptive meal prepared (v.9)

schools.summary: Rebekah clothes Jacob in Esau's garments: 'and Rebekah took goodly raiment of her eldest son Esau, which were with her in the house, and put them upon Jacob her younger son' — Rebekah dressing Jacob in Esau's best (goodly) garments, which she kept in the house (part of the disguise, that Jacob might seem Esau by smell and appearance, v.27, the mother completing the deception, the raiment of the firstborn put on the younger); Esau's 'goodly raiment' — his best clothes (perhaps the priestly/firstborn garments, by some, or simply his finest, the smell of which, of the field, v.27, would aid the deception, the disguise of the firstborn); 'put them upon Jacob her younger son' (the younger clothed as the elder, the mother's careful disguise, the deception of appearance and smell, v.27, the scheme advancing, with the skins, v.16) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Rebekah took goodly raiment of her eldest son Esau, which were with her in the house, and put them upon Jacob her younger son
Rebekah clothing Jacob in Esau's garments
the younger disguised as the elder, the garments whose smell (v.27) would aid the deception
the mother completing the disguise (with the skins, v.16)

schools.summary: Rebekah covers Jacob's hands and neck with goat-skins: 'and she put the skins of the kids of the goats upon his hands, and upon the smooth of his neck' — Rebekah covering Jacob's smooth hands and neck with the goat-skins (v.9), to imitate Esau's hairiness (v.11; 25:25, the device to deceive Isaac's touch, vv.21-23, the completing of the disguise, the deception of the blind father's feeling); the kid-skins (soft, with hair) used to mimic Esau's hairy arms and neck (the device addressing Jacob's objection, v.11, the deception of touch prepared); 'upon his hands, and upon the smooth of his neck' (the smooth parts covered to feel hairy, the careful imitation, the deception of Isaac's touch, vv.21-23, the disguise complete, raiment, smell, and now the hairy feel) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And she put the skins of the kids of the goats upon his hands, and upon the smooth of his neck
Rebekah covering Jacob's hands and neck with the goat-skins
the device imitating Esau's hairiness (v.11; 25:25) to deceive Isaac's touch
the disguise complete - raiment, smell, and the hairy feel

schools.summary: Rebekah hands Jacob the meat: 'and she gave the savoury meat and the bread, which she had prepared, into the hand of her son Jacob' — Rebekah giving Jacob the prepared meat and bread to carry to Isaac (the last step before the deception, the meal, the dressed kids, v.14, and bread put in Jacob's hand, the scheme ready to be executed); the meat and bread Rebekah had prepared (vv.9, 14) — the deceptive meal (the food to be brought to Isaac as Esau's venison, the device ready); 'into the hand of her son Jacob' (Jacob, now disguised, vv.15-16, and bearing the meat, sent to Isaac, the deception about to be carried out, vv.18-29, the mother's part done, the son's to begin, the scheme at its crisis) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And she gave the savoury meat and the bread, which she had prepared, into the hand of her son Jacob
Rebekah handing Jacob the meat to carry to Isaac
the disguised son (vv.15-16) sent with the deceptive meal
the scheme at its crisis, the deception about to be carried out (vv.18-29)

schools.summary: Jacob approaches Isaac to begin the deception: 'and he came unto his father, and said, My father: and he said, Here am I; who art thou, my son?' — Jacob, disguised (vv.15-16) and bearing the meat (v.17), coming to Isaac and addressing him (the beginning of the deception, the son approaching to personate his brother, the crisis of the scheme); Isaac's answer and question — 'Here am I; who art thou, my son?' — the blind father, uncertain (perhaps the voice, v.22, or the quick return, v.20, gave him pause), asking which son this was (the question that drew out Jacob's lie, v.19, the doubt that the deception would have to overcome); Jacob's coming and Isaac's question setting the deception in motion (vv.19-24, the blind father's uncertainty, the son about to lie, the scheme at its decisive point) — Gill, Henry.

themes: And he came unto his father, and said, My father: and he said, Here am I; who art thou, my son?
Jacob approaching Isaac to begin the deception
the disguised son beginning to personate Esau
the blind father's uncertain question drawing out the lie (v.19)

schools.summary: Jacob's lie: 'and Jacob said unto his father, I am Esau thy firstborn; I have done according as thou badest me: arise, I pray thee, sit and eat of my venison, that thy soul may bless me' — 'I am Esau thy firstborn' a palpable, plain falsehood — Jacob directly lying, claiming to be Esau (he was not), and 'thy firstborn' (he had bought the birthright, 25:33, yet was not the firstborn by birth, the lie cannot be vindicated nor excused, Clarke, Calmet, the attempts of some to excuse it, he had bought the birthright, the oracle was his, 'I am he that should receive the blessing,' failing — the words a direct, deliberate deception, a sin not to be palliated, the patriarch's plain guilt in the lie); Jacob adding further falsehoods — 'I have done as thou badest me' (he had not, it was the kids, not Esau's venison), 'eat of my venison' (it was goat, not venison, the deceit compounded, lie upon lie, the sad spectacle of the patriarch in the act of deception, the means, deceit, not to be confused with the end, the blessing, which was God's appointment, 25:23); the blessing itself, though got by sinful means, God's appointment (the elder to serve the younger, 25:23, Hebrews 11:20 saying 'by faith Isaac blessed Jacob' — the faith in the act of blessing, the prophetic conveying of the covenant, not in Jacob's lie, God overruling, but not approving, the deceit, the deception chastised in Jacob's long exile and sorrows, the bitter fruit of the sin, the witnesses holding both the certainty of God's purpose and the real guilt of the means) — Clarke, Calvin, Gill, Hebrews 11:20.

debates.question: How is Jacob's 'I am Esau thy firstborn' to be judged?

sides.position: A plain, deliberate falsehood, not to be vindicated nor excused - the attempts to palliate it (he bought the birthright; the oracle was his) fail; a sin compounded with further deceit
Yet the blessing itself was God's appointment (25:23; Hebrews 11:20, by faith Isaac blessed) - God overruling but not approving the means, which were chastised in Jacob's long sorrows

themes: And Jacob said unto his father, I am Esau thy firstborn... that thy soul may bless me
Jacob's lie (the crux of the deception)
a plain, deliberate falsehood, not to be excused, compounded with further deceit
yet the blessing God's appointment (25:23; Hebrews 11:20), God overruling but not approving the sin

schools.summary: Jacob uses God's name in the deception: 'and Isaac said unto his son, How is it that thou hast found it so quickly, my son? And he said, Because the LORD thy God brought it to me' — Isaac, suspecting (the venison came too quickly for a hunt), questioning Jacob ('How is it that thou hast found it so quickly, my son?', the blind father's doubt, the quick return that gave him pause, the suspicion the lie had to overcome); Jacob answering, 'Because the LORD thy God brought it to me' — a grievous aggravation of the deceit: Jacob using God's name to support the lie (ascribing the quick success to God's providence, the taking of the LORD's name in vain, Exodus 20:7, to further the deception, the sin compounded by bringing in God, the saddest part of the falsehood, and note 'the LORD thy God,' as if not yet fully his own, though some read it of Isaac's covenant-God); Jacob's bringing in God's name aggravating the lie — using religion to cloak deceit (the warning against the misuse of God's name, the sin not excused but the more grievous, the deception deepened) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And Isaac said unto his son, How is it that thou hast found it so quickly, my son? And he said, Because the LORD thy God brought it to me
Jacob using God's name in the deception
the grievous aggravation - cloaking the lie with God's name (Exodus 20:7)
the deception deepened, religion used to cloak deceit

schools.summary: Isaac's doubt and wish to feel Jacob: 'and Isaac said unto Jacob, Come near, I pray thee, that I may feel thee, my son, whether thou be my very son Esau or not' — Isaac, still doubtful (the voice, v.22, gave him pause), bidding Jacob come near that he might feel him (test by touch whether he was Esau, the hairy man, v.11, the blind father's test, the doubt the disguise, the goat-skins, v.16, would have to pass); Isaac's suspicion — he was not sure this was Esau (the voice unlike, v.22, the careful father testing, the doubt that the deception, the skins, v.16, and the raiment, v.15, was prepared to overcome); Isaac's wish to feel Jacob bringing the deception to its crisis (v.22) — the goat-skins to deceive the touch (the blind father relying on feeling, and smell, v.27, the test the disguise must pass, the deception at its decisive moment) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Isaac said unto Jacob, Come near, I pray thee, that I may feel thee, my son, whether thou be my very son Esau or not
Isaac's doubt and his wish to feel Jacob
the blind father testing by touch (v.11), the goat-skins (v.16) to deceive his feeling
the deception at its crisis, the disguise the doubt must overcome

schools.summary: Isaac's perplexity - the voice and the hands: 'and Jacob went near unto Isaac his father; and he felt him, and said, The voice is Jacob's voice, but the hands are the hands of Esau' — Isaac feeling Jacob (the goat-skins, v.16) and being perplexed: the voice he heard was Jacob's, but the hands he felt were Esau's (hairy, the conflict of his senses, the disguise deceiving the touch, but the voice betraying the truth, the blind father caught between the evidences); the voice was Jacob's — the one mark the disguise could not hide (the truth pressing on Isaac, yet the touch, and the lie, overcame the doubt); 'the hands are the hands of Esau' (the goat-skins, v.16, deceiving the touch, the false evidence overruling the true, the voice, Isaac's senses divided, the disguise prevailing, the famous saying marking the perplexity of the deceived father, a perennial figure of inconsistency — the voice of one, the hands of another) — Gill.

themes: And Jacob went near unto Isaac his father; and he felt him, and said, The voice is Jacob's voice, but the hands are the hands of Esau
Isaac's perplexity - the voice and the hands
the disguise (goat-skins, v.16) deceiving the touch, but the voice betraying the truth
the blind father caught between the true voice and the false hands

schools.summary: Isaac deceived by the hairy hands, blesses Jacob: 'and he discerned him not, because his hands were hairy, as his brother Esau's hands: so he blessed him' — Isaac failing to recognize Jacob, because the goat-skin hands (v.16) felt hairy like Esau's (the touch deceiving the doubt, v.22, the disguise prevailing, the blind father deceived); the hands felt like Esau's (hairy, v.11; 25:25) — the device (v.16) succeeding (the false evidence overruling the true, the voice, v.22, the deception complete as to the touch); 'so he blessed him' (Isaac, deceived, proceeding to bless Jacob, the blessing given, vv.27-29, the deception's aim attained, yet the blessing, though got by deceit, God's appointment, 25:23; Hebrews 11:20, by faith Isaac blessed Jacob, the deceit overruled to the appointed end) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB, Clarke.

themes: And he discerned him not, because his hands were hairy, as his brother Esau's hands: so he blessed him
Isaac deceived by the hairy hands, blessing Jacob
the goat-skin hands (v.16) deceiving the touch, the false evidence overruling the true voice
the blessing given (though by deceit), yet God's appointment (25:23; Hebrews 11:20)

schools.summary: Isaac's last question and Jacob's bold lie: 'and he said, Art thou my very son Esau? And he said, I am' — Isaac, still half-doubting (the voice, v.22), asking once more if this was truly Esau, and Jacob answering, 'I am' — a bold, direct reaffirmation of the lie (v.19, the deception persisted in, the patriarch's plain falsehood repeated); Isaac's lingering doubt — asking a second time (the voice still troubled him, the careful father pressing, the question Jacob answered with another lie); 'I am' (Jacob's bold, brief reaffirmation of the falsehood, v.19, the deception persisted in to the end, the sin compounded, the lie repeated under direct question, the deceit not to be excused, the blessing now to be given, v.25) — Gill.

themes: And he said, Art thou my very son Esau? And he said, I am
Isaac's last question and Jacob's bold lie
the lingering doubt (v.22) overcome by the bold reaffirmation of the falsehood (v.19)
the deceit persisted in, the sin compounded

schools.summary: Isaac eats the meal before the blessing: 'and he said, Bring it near to me, and I will eat of my son's venison, that my soul may bless thee. And he brought it near to him, and he did eat: and he brought him wine, and he drank' — Isaac, now satisfied (the doubt overcome, vv.22-24), bidding Jacob bring the meal, he would eat before blessing (as he had purposed, v.4, the deception of his taste, the goat dressed as venison, v.9, the meal preceding the blessing); Isaac eating the meat and drinking the wine — the blind father deceived in his taste (thinking it Esau's venison, the meal completed, the deception working through the appetite, the way to the blessing); Isaac's eating and drinking (the deceptive meal) preparing for the blessing (vv.26-29, the father, deceived in every sense, touch, taste, and soon smell, v.27, about to bless Jacob, the deception's aim near, the blessing to be given) — Gill, Keil and Delitzsch.

themes: And he said, Bring it near to me, and I will eat of my son's venison... and he did eat: and he brought him wine, and he drank
Isaac eating the meal before the blessing
the blind father deceived in his taste (the goat dressed as venison, v.9)
the meal preceding the blessing he had purposed (v.4)

schools.summary: Isaac calls Jacob to kiss him: 'and his father Isaac said unto him, Come near now, and kiss me, my son' — Isaac bidding Jacob come near and kiss him (the token of fatherly affection before the blessing, the kiss that brought Jacob near, and the smell of the raiment, v.27, that further deceived, the affectionate prelude to the blessing); the kiss — a mark of affection (and, in the patriarchal blessing, a solemn token, the drawing near that allowed the smell of Esau's garments, v.27, to reach Isaac, the affectionate gesture); 'my son' (Isaac's tender address, the affection of the father, deceived, yet loving, the kiss and the smell, v.27, the last of the deceived senses, the prelude to the blessing, vv.27-29) — Gill, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: And his father Isaac said unto him, Come near now, and kiss me, my son
Isaac calling Jacob to kiss him
the affectionate prelude to the blessing
the drawing near that let the smell of Esau's raiment (v.27) further deceive

schools.summary: Isaac, smelling the raiment, begins the blessing: 'and he came near, and kissed him: and he smelled the smell of his raiment, and blessed him, and said, See, the smell of my son is as the smell of a field which the LORD hath blessed' — as Jacob kissed him, Isaac smelling Esau's garments (v.15) — the scent of the field (the open country, herbs, the hunter's clothes, the last of the deceived senses, touch, taste, smell, the smell that confirmed, to the deceived father, that this was Esau); Isaac, moved by the smell, beginning the blessing: 'the smell of my son is as the smell of a field which the LORD hath blessed' (the fragrance of the field, the open, fruitful country, blessed of God, likened to the smell of the garments, the poetic opening of the blessing, the father's heart warmed, the blessing, vv.28-29, beginning); Isaac's blessing (vv.27-29), though given to the deceived father's mistaken son, a prophetic, Spirit-led act (Hebrews 11:20, by faith Isaac blessed — God overruling the deception so that the blessing fell on the appointed heir, Jacob, 25:23, the smell of the field a figure, by some, of the fruitfulness to come, v.28, the blessing of the covenant) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And he came near, and kissed him: and he smelled the smell of his raiment, and blessed him, and said, See, the smell of my son is as the smell of a field which the LORD hath blessed
Isaac, smelling the raiment, beginning the blessing
the scent of Esau's garments (v.15) opening the blessing
the prophetic, Spirit-led act (Hebrews 11:20) falling on the appointed heir (25:23)

schools.summary: The blessing of plenty: 'therefore God give thee of the dew of heaven, and the fatness of the earth, and plenty of corn and wine' — Isaac blessing Jacob with the dew of heaven (the rain and moisture that fertilize the land) and the fatness of the earth (its richness and fruitfulness, the blessing of abundance, the temporal bounty of the covenant, the land of promise, fruitful and blessed); 'plenty of corn and wine' (the abundance of the harvest, grain and the vine, the fruitfulness of the land, the temporal blessing, the prosperity promised to Jacob and his seed, Israel, the fruitful land); the blessing of dew, fatness, corn, and wine — the temporal abundance of the covenant (the land flowing with plenty, Deuteronomy 33:28, the fruitfulness of Israel in the land of promise, the bounty that was Jacob's, and his seed's, the chief blessing falling on the appointed heir, 25:23, the prophetic blessing of plenty) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: Therefore God give thee of the dew of heaven, and the fatness of the earth, and plenty of corn and wine
the blessing of plenty - dew, fatness, corn, and wine
the temporal abundance of the covenant, the fruitful land of promise (Deuteronomy 33:28)
the chief blessing falling on the appointed heir (25:23)

schools.summary: The blessing of dominion and the covenant-formula: 'let people serve thee, and nations bow down to thee: be lord over thy brethren, and let thy mother's sons bow down to thee: cursed be every one that curseth thee, and blessed be he that blesseth thee' — Isaac blessing Jacob with dominion: peoples and nations to serve him, and lordship over his brethren (the fulfillment of the oracle, 25:23, the elder shall serve the younger, the rule of Jacob/Israel over the nations, and over Edom, 2 Samuel 8:14, the dominion of the covenant-seed); 'thy mother's sons bow down to thee' (the lordship over Esau, and any other brethren, the elder serving the younger, 25:23, confirmed, Israel's dominion over Edom, the precedence of Jacob); 'cursed be every one that curseth thee, and blessed be he that blesseth thee' the very formula of the Abrahamic covenant (12:3, I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee) — now conveyed to Jacob (the covenant-blessing, and the seed, Christ, in whom all nations are blessed, confirmed to the appointed heir, the chief, covenant blessing, not merely temporal, falling on Jacob, the line of promise secured in him, 25:23; Romans 9) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Let people serve thee, and nations bow down to thee: be lord over thy brethren... cursed be every one that curseth thee, and blessed be he that blesseth thee
the blessing of dominion and the covenant-formula
the dominion (the elder serving the younger, 25:23; 2 Samuel 8:14)
the Abrahamic covenant (12:3) conveyed to Jacob, the line of promise secured in the appointed heir

schools.summary: Esau returns just after the blessing: 'and it came to pass, as soon as Isaac had made an end of blessing Jacob, and Jacob was yet scarce gone out from the presence of Isaac his father, that Esau his brother came in from his hunting' — Esau returning at the very moment the blessing was finished and Jacob just gone (the narrow timing, the providence ordering it so that the blessing was secured before Esau came, the dramatic juncture, the deception accomplished just in time); how narrowly Jacob escaped — barely gone before Esau entered (the providence, and Rebekah's haste, securing the blessing, the close timing); Esau coming in with his venison (v.31), about to discover the deception (the bitter scene to follow, vv.32-38, the providence that had given the blessing to Jacob before Esau's return, the elder come too late) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass, as soon as Isaac had made an end of blessing Jacob, and Jacob was yet scarce gone out... that Esau his brother came in from his hunting
Esau returning just after the blessing
the narrow timing, the blessing secured for Jacob before Esau's return
the elder come too late, the bitter discovery at hand (vv.32-38)

schools.summary: Esau brings his venison to be blessed: 'and he also had made savoury meat, and brought it unto his father, and said unto his father, Let my father arise, and eat of his son's venison, that thy soul may bless me' — Esau, having hunted and dressed the venison (as Isaac bade, vv.3-4), bringing it and asking his father to eat and bless him (the unsuspecting Esau, come for the blessing he thought his, the dramatic moment before the discovery, v.32); Esau bringing the true venison (the game he had hunted) — in contrast to the goat Jacob had brought (v.9, the unsuspecting son, come too late, the meal offered); 'that thy soul may bless me' (Esau asking for the blessing, the very words Jacob had used, v.19, the elder come for the blessing already given to the younger, the bitter discovery at hand, vv.32-33, the unsuspecting Esau about to learn the truth) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And he also had made savoury meat, and brought it unto his father... that thy soul may bless me
Esau bringing his venison to be blessed
the unsuspecting elder come too late for the blessing already given to Jacob
the bitter discovery at hand (vv.32-33)

schools.summary: The discovery beginning - Esau names himself: 'and Isaac his father said unto him, Who art thou? And he said, I am thy son, thy firstborn Esau' — Isaac, startled (a second son claiming to be Esau), asking who he was, and Esau answering plainly, 'I am thy son, thy firstborn Esau' (the truth now breaking, the discovery beginning, the deception about to be exposed); Isaac's question — the dawning realization that he had been deceived (another claiming to be Esau, the alarm of the father, the question that brought the truth); 'I am thy son, thy firstborn Esau' (the real Esau naming himself, in contrast to Jacob's lie, v.19, the truth that exposed the deception, the discovery that would make Isaac tremble, v.33, the elder come for the blessing already lost) — Gill.

themes: And Isaac his father said unto him, Who art thou? And he said, I am thy son, thy firstborn Esau
the discovery beginning - Esau names himself
the truth breaking (against Jacob's lie, v.19)
the deception about to be exposed (v.33)

schools.summary: Isaac trembles and confirms the blessing (its irrevocability): 'and Isaac trembled very exceedingly, and said, Who? where is he that hath taken venison, and brought it me, and I have eaten of all before thou camest, and have blessed him? yea, and he shall be blessed' — 'Isaac trembled very exceedingly' (with a great trembling) — the patriarch shaking violently, realizing he had been deceived, yet the trembling chiefly at the overruling providence of God — Isaac perceiving that, against his own purpose to bless Esau (v.4), God's hand had directed the blessing to Jacob (the appointed heir, 25:23, the awe at God's secret counsel overruling his intent, the recognition that the blessing had fallen where God meant it); Isaac realizing he had eaten and blessed another (Jacob, not Esau, the deception exposed, the patriarch undeceived, yet, instead of revoking the blessing, confirming it); the great word — 'yea, and he shall be blessed': Isaac, perceiving God's hand, not revoking but confirming the blessing to Jacob (the irrevocability of the blessing, a prophetic, Spirit-led act, not to be recalled, Isaac now acquiescing in God's purpose, the elder serving the younger, 25:23, and, by faith, Hebrews 11:20, by faith Isaac blessed Jacob... concerning things to come, ratifying it, the certainty of the election — the blessing, once given, stood, for it was God's, the trembling turned to faith and acquiescence) — Clarke, Calvin, Gill, Hebrews 11:20.

debates.question: Why did Isaac tremble and confirm the blessing ('he shall be blessed')?

sides.position: At the overruling providence of God - perceiving the blessing had fallen, against his own purpose, on the appointed heir (25:23); confirming it irrevocably, acquiescing by faith (Hebrews 11:20) in God's purpose, the certainty of the election

themes: And Isaac trembled very exceedingly... yea, and he shall be blessed
Isaac's trembling and his confirming the blessing (the crux of its irrevocability)
the awe at God's overruling providence (the blessing fallen on the appointed heir, 25:23, against Isaac's purpose)
the irrevocable confirming, Isaac acquiescing by faith (Hebrews 11:20), the certainty of the election

schools.summary: Esau's great and bitter cry: 'and when Esau heard the words of his father, he cried with a great and exceeding bitter cry, and said unto his father, Bless me, even me also, O my father' — Esau, learning that the blessing was irrevocably Jacob's (v.33), breaking into a great, bitter cry (the anguish of the disappointed elder, the grief at the lost blessing, the bitter cry, Hebrews 12:17, he sought it with tears, the realization that the blessing could not be his); the depth of Esau's grief — a great, bitter cry (the anguish of the man who had despised the birthright, 25:34, now bewailing the lost blessing, the bitterness of the disappointment); 'Bless me, even me also, O my father' (Esau begging for a blessing, the plea of the disappointed son, the desire for some blessing, vv.36, 38, the cry that moved Isaac to give the secondary blessing, vv.39-40, but the chief blessing was gone) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And when Esau heard the words of his father, he cried with a great and exceeding bitter cry, and said unto his father, Bless me, even me also, O my father
Esau's great and bitter cry
the anguish of the disappointed elder at the lost blessing (Hebrews 12:17)
the man who despised the birthright (25:34) now bewailing it, begging for a blessing

schools.summary: Isaac tells Esau of Jacob's subtilty: 'and he said, Thy brother came with subtilty, and hath taken away thy blessing' — Isaac telling Esau plainly that Jacob had come with subtilty (craft, deceit) and taken away the blessing (the patriarch owning the deception, Jacob's guile, vv.18-29, the blessing now Jacob's, the truth of the subtilty acknowledged); Isaac calling it 'subtilty' (deceit, guile) — owning the wrong of Jacob's means (the deception, the patriarch not excusing the deceit, yet not revoking the blessing, v.33, the guile acknowledged); 'hath taken away thy blessing' (the blessing Esau thought his now Jacob's, the elder dispossessed, by Jacob's guile, yet by God's purpose, 25:23, the loss that occasioned Esau's bitter complaint, v.36) — Gill, Clarke, JFB.

themes: And he said, Thy brother came with subtilty, and hath taken away thy blessing
Isaac telling Esau of Jacob's subtilty
the patriarch owning Jacob's guile (vv.18-29), not excusing the deceit
the elder dispossessed (by deceit, yet by God's purpose, 25:23)

schools.summary: Esau's complaint of the two supplantings and the name Jacob: 'and he said, Is not he rightly named Jacob? for he hath supplanted me these two times: he took away my birthright; and, behold, now he hath taken away my blessing. And he said, Hast thou not reserved a blessing for me?' — Esau playing bitterly on Jacob's name ('Jacob,' heel-holder, supplanter, 25:26): rightly named, for he had supplanted (tripped up, overreached) Esau twice (the name fitting the deeds, the bitter wordplay of the wronged elder); Esau's two complaints — the birthright (25:31-34, which Esau sold, yet now bewails) and the blessing (vv.18-29, the two supplantings, yet Esau himself had despised and sold the birthright, 25:34, so his complaint not wholly just, the elder dispossessed of both); 'Hast thou not reserved a blessing for me?' (Esau begging for some blessing, the plea of the disappointed son, the desire for a remnant of blessing, the secondary blessing, vv.39-40, the chief blessing gone) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Is not he rightly named Jacob? for he hath supplanted me these two times: he took away my birthright; and... now he hath taken away my blessing
Esau's complaint of the two supplantings and the name Jacob
the bitter wordplay (Jacob, supplanter, 25:26), the birthright (which Esau sold, 25:34) and the blessing taken
the plea for some blessing reserved (vv.39-40)

schools.summary: Isaac's answer that the chief blessing was Jacob's: 'and Isaac answered and said unto Esau, Behold, I have made him thy lord, and all his brethren have I given to him for servants; and with corn and wine have I sustained him: and what shall I do now unto thee, my son?' — Isaac telling Esau that he had given Jacob the lordship (dominion over his brethren, v.29, the elder to serve the younger, 25:23, the chief blessing, the dominion, was Jacob's, Esau to serve him, the blessing irrevocable, v.33); 'with corn and wine have I sustained him' (the blessing of plenty, v.28, given to Jacob, the temporal abundance and the dominion both Jacob's, the chief blessing bestowed); 'what shall I do now unto thee, my son?' (Isaac, the chief blessing given, asking what remained for Esau, the patriarch's tender perplexity, the chief blessing gone, the dominion and the covenant, only a lesser blessing, vv.39-40, remaining, the elder dispossessed) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Isaac answered and said unto Esau, Behold, I have made him thy lord... and what shall I do now unto thee, my son?
Isaac's answer that the chief blessing was given to Jacob
the lordship (25:23; v.29) and plenty (v.28) Jacob's, the blessing irrevocable
the chief blessing gone, only a lesser remaining for Esau (vv.39-40)

schools.summary: Esau's tears: 'and Esau said unto his father, Hast thou but one blessing, my father? bless me, even me also, O my father. And Esau lifted up his voice, and wept' — Esau pleading that surely his father had more than one blessing, begging for a blessing for himself (the desperate plea of the disappointed elder, the desire for some portion, the secondary blessing, vv.39-40); 'Esau lifted up his voice, and wept' — Esau weeping with loud lamentations for the lost blessing, the Apostle taking this up (Hebrews 12:16-17): Esau, 'a profane person... afterward, when he would have inherited the blessing, was rejected: for he found no place of repentance, though he sought it carefully with tears' (the meaning: Esau's tears not the tears of true repentance, for his profanity, 25:34, but of bitter regret for the lost temporal blessing, he finding no way to reverse the rejection, no place of repentance — either no way to change his father's mind / the irrevocable blessing, or no genuine repentance in himself, the warning against the profane heart that weeps for consequences, not for sin, the bitter, too-late tears of the worldly man); Esau's tears, though great, for the earthly blessing (the fatness, the dominion), not for the spiritual loss he had despised (25:34, the warning, Hebrews 12:16-17, against selling the spiritual for the temporal and then weeping too late, the profane man's regret without true repentance) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

sides.position: Bitter regret for the lost temporal blessing, not true repentance for his profanity (25:34) - the profane man found no place of repentance, though he sought it with tears; the warning against weeping for consequences, not sin

themes: And Esau said unto his father, Hast thou but one blessing, my father?... And Esau lifted up his voice, and wept
Esau's tears (the crux of his weeping)
loud tears for the lost temporal blessing, not true repentance for his profanity (25:34)
the Apostle's warning - the profane Esau found no place of repentance, though he sought it with tears (Hebrews 12:16-17)

schools.summary: Esau's secondary blessing of dwelling: 'and Isaac his father answered and said unto him, Behold, thy dwelling shall be the fatness of the earth, and of the dew of heaven from above' — Isaac giving Esau a lesser blessing: his dwelling (the land of Edom/Seir) would have some fatness and dew (some fertility), yet the phrase read two ways — (a) Esau's dwelling shall be of the fatness (a portion of fertility, a lesser echo of Jacob's blessing, v.28), or (b) 'away from the fatness' (the Hebrew preposition can mean 'from/away from') — Esau's dwelling apart from the fatness and dew (the rugged, less fertile land of Edom), the dominant reading taking it as a lesser, qualified blessing (Edom not without fertility, yet inferior to Jacob's portion); the echo of Jacob's blessing (v.28) — yet inferior (Esau's portion, Edom, some fertility, but not the chief blessing, the covenant, the dominion, the secondary blessing, a faint shadow of the first); Esau's blessing a prophecy of Edom — its land and destiny (vv.39-40, the lesser portion, the descendants of Esau, Edom, given a dwelling, yet under Jacob, v.40, the prophetic, qualified blessing) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What is Esau's blessing - 'of the fatness' or 'away from the fatness'?

sides.position: A lesser, qualified blessing - Esau's land (Edom) given some fertility, yet inferior to Jacob's portion (v.28); a faint echo of the first blessing
'Away from the fatness' - Esau's dwelling apart from the fatness and dew, the rugged, less fertile land of Edom

themes: And Isaac his father answered and said unto him, Behold, thy dwelling shall be the fatness of the earth, and of the dew of heaven from above
Esau's secondary blessing of dwelling
'of the fatness' (a lesser echo of Jacob's blessing, v.28) vs 'away from the fatness' (the rugged land of Edom)
the prophecy of Edom, a qualified, inferior portion (vv.39-40)

schools.summary: The prophecy of Edom - the sword, service, and broken yoke: 'and by thy sword shalt thou live, and shalt serve thy brother; and it shall come to pass when thou shalt have the dominion, that thou shalt break his yoke from off thy neck' — Esau (and Edom, his descendants) living by the sword (war, plunder, the warlike, predatory life of Edom, the prophecy of the nation's character, the rough, martial life of the Edomites); 'shalt serve thy brother' (Edom serving Jacob/Israel, the fulfillment of the oracle, 25:23, the elder serving the younger, Edom subdued by David, 2 Samuel 8:14, the subjection of Esau's line to Jacob's, the dominion of Israel over Edom); 'when thou shalt have the dominion, that thou shalt break his yoke from off thy neck' (yet Edom would, in time, break free, revolt from Judah, as under Joram, 2 Kings 8:20-22, the prophecy of Edom's later independence, the yoke of Israel broken, the qualified subjection, serving, yet at times throwing off the yoke, the prophetic history of Edom) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And by thy sword shalt thou live, and shalt serve thy brother; and it shall come to pass when thou shalt have the dominion, that thou shalt break his yoke from off thy neck
the prophecy of Edom - the sword, the service, and the broken yoke
Edom warlike, subject to Israel (25:23; 2 Samuel 8:14)
yet in time revolting, breaking the yoke (2 Kings 8:20-22)

schools.summary: Esau's hatred and purpose to kill Jacob: 'and Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing wherewith his father blessed him: and Esau said in his heart, The days of mourning for my father are at hand; then will I slay my brother Jacob' — Esau hating Jacob for taking the blessing (vv.27-29, the envy and bitterness turned to hatred, the murderous resentment of the dispossessed elder, the hatred that would drive Jacob into exile, vv.42-45); Esau saying in his heart, 'The days of mourning for my father are at hand' (Esau, supposing Isaac soon to die, v.2, planning to kill Jacob after his father's death, to spare his father grief, or to wait the mourning, the cold, deliberate plot, the purpose of fratricide, as Cain, 4:8); 'then will I slay my brother Jacob' (Esau resolving on fratricide, the murderous hatred, 1 John 3:15, he that hateth his brother is a murderer, the bitter fruit of the deception and the lost blessing, the danger that Rebekah's foresight, vv.42-45, would avert by sending Jacob away) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing wherewith his father blessed him... then will I slay my brother Jacob
Esau's hatred and his purpose to kill Jacob
the murderous resentment of the dispossessed elder (1 John 3:15)
the plot of fratricide (cf. 4:8), the danger Rebekah would avert (vv.42-45)

schools.summary: Rebekah warns Jacob of Esau's purpose: 'and these words of Esau her elder son were told to Rebekah: and she sent and called Jacob her younger son, and said unto him, Behold, thy brother Esau, as touching thee, doth comfort himself, purposing to kill thee' — Esau's murderous plot (v.41) coming to Rebekah's ears, the mother, alarmed, calling Jacob (the providence, and Rebekah's vigilance, that warned Jacob in time, the danger disclosed); Rebekah telling Jacob that Esau 'doth comfort himself, purposing to kill thee' (Esau soothing his grief with the thought of revenge, the murder, v.41, the threat to Jacob's life, the mother warning her son of the danger, the plot disclosed); Rebekah's warning leading to her plan to send Jacob away (vv.43-45, the mother, who had schemed the deception, now must send her favored son into exile, the bitter fruit of the deceit, the danger that drove Jacob to Laban, and the long separation) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And these words of Esau her elder son were told to Rebekah... Behold, thy brother Esau... doth comfort himself, purposing to kill thee
Rebekah warning Jacob of Esau's purpose
the mother, learning the plot (v.41), warning Jacob in time
the warning leading to the flight (vv.43-45), the bitter fruit of the deceit

schools.summary: Rebekah bids Jacob flee to Laban: 'now therefore, my son, obey my voice; and arise, flee thou to Laban my brother to Haran' — Rebekah bidding Jacob flee to her brother Laban in Haran (Padan-aram, the kindred, 24:10, the mother sending her son away for safety, from Esau's murder, v.41, the flight to the kindred, the providence ordering Jacob's exile, where he would also find a wife, 28:2; chs. 29-31); the refuge with Laban (Rebekah's brother, 24:29) in Haran — safety from Esau (the kindred's house, the place of Jacob's long sojourn, chs. 29-31); 'to Haran' (the place of the kindred, the family of Nahor, 24:10; 11:31, Jacob sent there for refuge, and, by Isaac's later charge, 28:2, for a wife, the providence overruling the flight for good, the meeting with Rachel, the building of the house of Israel, the exile that was also the way of blessing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Now therefore, my son, obey my voice; and arise, flee thou to Laban my brother to Haran
Rebekah bidding Jacob flee to Laban
the mother sending Jacob for safety to the kindred (24:10, 29)
the exile that providence would overrule for good (chs. 29-31)

schools.summary: Rebekah's hope that the danger would pass quickly: 'and tarry with him a few days, until thy brother's fury turn away' — Rebekah hoping Jacob would need to stay only 'a few days' (a short time) until Esau's fury cooled (the mother's hope of a quick return, the supposed brevity of the exile, which proved to be twenty years, 31:38, the plan to wait out Esau's anger); 'a few days' (Rebekah's hope of a short separation, the supposed brevity, yet Jacob's exile was long, and Rebekah, it seems, never saw him again, the mother's expectation); 'until thy brother's fury turn away' (the plan to wait until Esau's hot anger, v.41, subsided, the hope that time would cool the wrath, the supposed temporary flight, the providence that would make the exile long and fruitful, chs. 29-31) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And tarry with him a few days, until thy brother's fury turn away
Rebekah's hope that the danger would pass quickly
the supposed brevity of the exile (which proved twenty years, 31:38)
the plan to wait out Esau's anger (v.41)

schools.summary: Rebekah's plan to fetch Jacob and her fear of losing both: 'until thy brother's anger turn away from thee, and he forget that which thou hast done to him: then I will send, and fetch thee from thence: why should I be deprived also of you both in one day?' — Rebekah hoping Esau's anger would pass and he would forget the wrong, and then she would send for Jacob ('then I will send, and fetch thee from thence,' the mother's plan to recall him when safe, the hope of reconciliation and return); Rebekah's plan to fetch Jacob back when the danger passed (the hope of his return, which she would not live to see, the mother's expectation of recall); 'why should I be deprived also of you both in one day?' (Rebekah fearing that if Esau killed Jacob, she would lose both sons in one day, Jacob slain, and Esau, the murderer, forfeit, fled, or executed, as Cain, 4:12, or by the avenger of blood, the mother's dread of a double bereavement, the fear that moved her to send Jacob away, the bitter fruit of the family's divisions and the deceit) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: Until thy brother's anger turn away from thee... then I will send, and fetch thee from thence: why should I be deprived also of you both in one day?
Rebekah's plan to fetch Jacob and her fear of losing both sons
the hope of recall when Esau's anger passed (which she would not live to see)
the dread of a double bereavement (Jacob slain, Esau forfeit, cf. 4:12), the bitter fruit of the deceit

schools.summary: Rebekah's pretext to Isaac for sending Jacob away: 'and Rebekah said to Isaac, I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Heth: if Jacob take a wife of the daughters of Heth, such as these which are of the daughters of the land, what good shall my life do me?' — Rebekah complaining to Isaac of Esau's Hittite wives (26:34-35, a grief of mind, the daughters of Heth, the Canaanites, a vexation, the mother's true grief at the Canaanite marriages, used now as the occasion, and pretext, for sending Jacob away); Rebekah urging that Jacob must not marry a Hittite (as Esau had, 26:34, the real concern, a wife of the kindred and faith, not the Canaanites, 24:3, the pretext, true in itself, by which she moved Isaac to send Jacob to Laban, 28:1-2, concealing the real reason, Esau's murder-plot, v.41, under the genuine concern for Jacob's marriage, the mother's prudence, and partial concealment); 'what good shall my life do me?' (Rebekah professing that life would be wearisome if Jacob too married a Canaanite, the design to have Isaac send Jacob to the kindred, 28:1-2, the true concern for the covenant-marriage joined with the hidden purpose of safety, the providence overruling all to send Jacob to Padan-aram, 28:2; chs. 29-31) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Rebekah said to Isaac, I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Heth... what good shall my life do me?
Rebekah's pretext to Isaac for sending Jacob away
the genuine grief at Esau's Canaanite wives (26:34-35) and concern for a wife of the kindred (24:3)
used to move Isaac to send Jacob to Laban (28:1-2), concealing the murder-plot (v.41)
